$$ model_{d,y} = z_{d,y} f_{d,y} + (1 – z_{d,y}) g_{d,y} $$

where $z_{d,y}$ is an indicator function (see below). $f_{d,y}$ and $g_{d,y}$ are also explained below.

Days ($d$) were specified as d = 0 to 90, where days were counted from 12:00 a.m. on 1 December, and we added an abundance of 0 whales passing for day 0 and 90 to anchor the fitted model [@buckland_estimated_1993].

Estimates were derived from either of two competing models ('Common' ($f$) and 'Specific' ($g$), e.g., [@li_baystdetect_2012]), describing changes in abundance across each annual migration. The model contributing each daily estimate was indicated using stochastic binary indicator variables $z_{d,y}$, each assigned a Bernoulli(0.5) prior distribution. As such, the posterior distribution of each $z_{d,y}$ indicated the probability of a daily estimate conforming to the common trend, allowing flexibility for departures from this trend that may only exist on certain days in certain years to be identified and modeled (rather than assuming all counts from an entire year conform to or depart from a common trend). The total number of whales passing at the survey location during each migration was then estimated by summing the expected value from the model averaged number of whales passing each day ($d$) from 0 to 90 (Laake et al., 2012).

These estimates were then rescaled to account for the differential passage rate at night [@perryman_diel_1999], based on the nine hour day multiplicative correction factor [@rugh_estimates_2005]. Specifically, we applied a constant night time correction factor that was assumed to be a Normally distributed fixed effect with mean of 1.0875 and standard deviation of 0.037 [@perryman_diel_1999].

For the 'Common model' ($f_{d,y}$), we assumed a typical trend in abundance throughout each annual migration (e.g. Buckland et al., 1993), with abundance changes assumed Normally distributed around a migration mid-point. A Normal distribution was specified as a quadratic function of days, on the log scale:

$$f_{d,y} = a_y + b_y *d_y + c_y * d^2_y$$

where the mid-point of the migration curve for each season $y$ was derived by $–b_y/2a_y$. This assumed common migration curve allowed information to be 'borrowed' across years when needed, specifying association across years to strengthen inference about migration curves in years with relatively sparse counts. Each parameter was specified to be drawn from a hierarchical Normal distribution.

$$ a_y \sim Normal(\mu_a, \sigma_a)$$

$$ b_y \sim Normal(\mu_b, \sigma_b)$$

$$ c_y \sim Normal(\mu_c, \sigma_c)$$

and $\mu \sim Normal(0, 10)$ and $\sigma \sim Uniform(0, 10)$, for $a$, $b$, and $c$.

This hierarchical and random effects approach allowed the timing, level and extent of the Normal migration curve to vary annually around the general pattern, if supported by the data.

To acknowledge and incorporate deviations from the common Normal model, the selection of an alternative 'specific' migration model was allowed ($g_{d,y}$). The 'specific' model was a semi-parametric model that estimated the time trends independently for each season without making any prior assumptions about its form (e.g., Laake et al., 2012). In this model, the shape of the relationship of true abundance across days was determined by the data via penalized splines [@ruppert_selecting_2002].

A linear (on the log scale) penalized spline was used to describe this relationship [@crainiceanu_bayesian_2005]:

$$ g_{d,y} = S_{0,y} + S_{1,y} * d_y + \sum_{k=1}^{m} \lambda_{k,y} (d_y – \kappa_{k,y}) $$ Where $S_{0,y}, S_{1,y}$ and $\lambda_{1,y}, \dots, \lambda_{m,y}$ were regression coefficients to be estimated separately for each year and $\kappa_{1,y} < \kappa_{2,y} < \dots < \kappa_{m,y}$ were fixed knots. To ensure the desired flexibility, we used m = 15 knots, which is a relatively large number. To avoid overfitting, the $\lambda$'s were penalized by assuming that they were Normally distributed random variables with mean 0 and standard deviation $\sim Uniform(0,10)$. The parameters $S_{0,y}, S_{1,y}$ were modeled as fixed effects with Normal(0, 10) prior distributions.

\newpage

## Richards function {.unnumbered}

The non-abbreviated Richards function contains an additional parameter ($\gamma$) and two peak parameters ($P_1$ and $P_2$). The $\gamma$ parameter controls the "flatness" at the peak of the function. In the current analysis, $\gamma = 1$ and $P_1 = P_2 = P$ to avoid overparameterization of the function. With more data, i.e., the number of observation days per season, these parameters may be estimated.

$$ C_{1,d} = \left( 1 + (2e^\gamma - 1) e^{\frac{P_1 - d}{-S_1}} \right)^{-\frac{1}{e^\gamma}} $$

$$ C_{2,d} = \left( 1 + (2e^\gamma - 1) e^{\frac{P_2 - d}{S_2}} \right)^{-\frac{1}{e^\gamma}} $$


```{r theta, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
S1 <- 2.5
S2 <- 2.5
theta <- c(0.01, 0.1, 1, 2, 4, 8)
P <- 40
max.N <- 800

true.mean.N <- matrix(data = 0, nrow = 90, 
                           ncol = length(theta))

for (c in 1:length(theta)){
  for (d in 1:90){
    true.mean.N[d, c] <- floor(Richards_fcn(d = d, 
                                            S1 = -S1, 
                                            S2 = S2,
                                            K = theta[c], 
                                            P = P, 
                                            min = 0, max = max.N)  )
    
  }
  
}

data.df <- data.frame(Day = rep(1:90, times = length(theta)),
                      mean.N = as.vector(true.mean.N),
                      theta = rep(theta, each = 90))

p.theta <- ggplot(data = data.df) +
  geom_path(aes(x = Day, y = mean.N), color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ theta) +
  ylab("")

save.fig.fcn(p.theta, 
             file.name = paste0("figures/theta_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"), 
             height = 5, width = 7,replace = T, dpi = dpi.set)



```

```{r plot-theta-effects, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
knitr::include_graphics(paste0("figures/theta_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"))
fig.P.num <- run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", 
                          pre_label = "Figure A", 
                          bkm = "Figure-theta",
                          start_at = 1)

```

::: {custom-style="Image Caption"}
`r run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", pre_label = "Figure A", bkm = "Figure-theta")` Effects of $\gamma$ on the shape of the Richards equation. In this example, $S_1$ = `r S1`, $S_2$ = `r S2`, $P_1$ = `r P`, $P_2$ = `r P`, $N_{max}$ = `r max.N`.
:::

\newpage

## Hyperparameter definitions {.unnumbered}

```{r table-hyperparameters, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}

# ft.hyper.params %>%
#   set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
#   #autofit() %>%
#   paginate(init = TRUE, hdr_ftr = TRUE) 


ft.hyper.params %>%
  # 1. Use Word's native autofit instead of R's autofit()
  # This prevents the calculation error that causes the crash
  #set_table_properties(layout = "autofit") %>%
  
  # 2. Set pagination
  paginate(init = TRUE, hdr_ftr = TRUE) %>% 
  
  # 3. Add caption last
  set_caption(
    caption = as_paragraph(
      "Definitions of hyperdistributions and their parameters. Normal distributions are defined with mean and variance. T(0,) indicates the truncation function that eliminates the negative part of a distribution."),
    fp_p = officer::fp_par(text.align = "left"),
    align_with_table = FALSE,
    autonum = run_autonum(
      seq_id = "tabA", 
      bkm = "hyperparameter-table",
      pre_label = "Table A"
    ),
    style = "Table Caption"
  )


```

\newpage

## Observer random effects {.unnumbered}

```{r plot-OBS-posteriors, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("figures/Observer_dens_", ver, ".png"))

```

::: {custom-style="Image Caption"}
`r run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", pre_label = "Figure A", bkm = "OBS-posteriors")` Marginal posterior density distributions of observer fixed effects. The 10 observers with the most sightings were used as random observer effects, whereas the remaining observers were pooled as a single observer effect.
:::

\newpage

## Changes in $S_1$ over time {.unnumbered}

```{r plot-S1-posteriors, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("figures/posteriors_S1_", ver, "_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"))
```

::: {custom-style="Image Caption"}
`r run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", pre_label = "Figure A", bkm = "S1-posteriors")` The marginal posterior distribution of the season-specific $S_1$ parameter of the Richards equation. $S_1$ defines the width of the right side of the curve.
:::

\newpage

## Changes in $S_2$ over time {.unnumbered}

```{r plot-S2-posteriors, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("figures/posteriors_S2_", ver, "_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"))
```

::: {custom-style="Image Caption"}
`r run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", pre_label = "Figure A", bkm = "S2-posteriors")` Marginal posterior distributions of season-specific $S_2$ parameters $S_2$ defines the width of the left side of the curve.
:::

\newpage

## Posterior distribution of $\theta$ {.unnumbered}

```{r plot-r-posterior, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("figures/posterior_r_", ver, "_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"))
```

::: {custom-style="Image Caption"}
`r run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", pre_label = "Figure A", bkm = "r-posterior")` The marginal posterior distribution of the $\theta$ parameter of the Negative Binomial likelihood. The $\theta$ parameter of a Negative Binomial distribution defines overdispersion (i.e., larger variance than expected), where small $\theta$ values indicate greater overdispersion.
:::

\newpage

## Characteristics of the Richards equation {.unnumbered}

### Effects of $S_1$ {.unnumbered}

```{r S1, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
S1 <- c(10, 5, 2.5, 1.2, 0.6, 0.3)
S2 <- 2.5
K <- 1
P <- 40
max.N <- 800
max.d <- 100

true.mean.N <- matrix(data = 0, nrow = max.d, ncol = length(S1))

for (c in 1:length(S1)){
  for (d in 1:max.d){
    true.mean.N[d, c] <- floor(Richards_fcn(d = d, 
                                            S1 = -S1[c], 
                                            S2 = S2,
                                            K = K, 
                                            P = P, 
                                            min = 0, max = max.N)  )
    

  }
  
}

data.df <- data.frame(Day = rep(1:max.d, times = length(S1)),
                      mean.N = as.vector(true.mean.N),
                      S1 = rep(S1, each = max.d))

p.S1 <- ggplot(data = data.df) +
  geom_path(aes(x = Day, y = mean.N), color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ S1) +
  xlab("Ordinal Day of Season") +
  ylab("") +
  theme_bw()

save.fig.fcn(p.S1, 
             file.name = paste0("figures/S1_", 
                                dpi.set, "dpi.png"), 
             height = 5, width = 7,replace = T, dpi = dpi.set)

```

```{r plot-S1-effects, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("figures/S1_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"))

```

::: {custom-style="Image Caption"}
`r library(officer); run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", pre_label = "Figure A", bkm = "Figure-S1")` Effects of $S_1$ on the shape of the Richards equation. In this example, $S_2$ = `r S2`, $\gamma$ = `r K`, $P$ = `r P`, $N_{max}$ = `r max.N`.
:::

\newpage

### Effects of $S_2$ {.unnumbered}

```{r S2, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
S1 <- 4.0
S2 <- c(0.3, 0.6, 1.2, 2.5, 5, 10)
K <- 1
P <- 40
max.N <- 800
max.d <- 100


true.mean.N <- matrix(data = 0, nrow = max.d, ncol = length(S2))

for (c in 1:length(S2)){
  for (d in 1:max.d){
    true.mean.N[d, c] <- floor(Richards_fcn(d = d, 
                                            S1 = -S1, 
                                            S2 = S2[c],
                                            K = K, 
                                            P = P, 
                                            min = 0, max = max.N)  )
    
  }
  
}

data.df <- data.frame(Day = rep(1:max.d, times = length(S2)),
                      mean.N = as.vector(true.mean.N),
                      S2 = rep(S2, each = max.d))

p.S2 <- ggplot(data = data.df) +
  geom_path(aes(x = Day, y = mean.N), color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ S2) +
  xlab("Ordinary Day of Season") +
  ylab("") +
  theme_bw()

save.fig.fcn(p.S2, 
             file.name = paste0("figures/S2_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"), 
             height = 5, width = 7,replace = T, dpi = dpi.set)


```

```{r plot-S2-effects, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("figures/S2_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"))

```

::: {custom-style="Image Caption"}
`r library(officer); run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", pre_label = "Figure A", bkm = "Figure-S2")` Effects of $S_2$ on the shape of the Richards equation. In this example, $S_1$ = `r S1`, $\gamma$ = `r K`, $P$ = `r P`, $N_{max}$ = `r max.N`.
:::

\newpage

### Effects of P {.unnumbered}

```{r P, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}
S1 <- 4.0
S2 <- 2.5
K <- 1.0
P <- c(20, 40, 60, 80)
max.N <- 800
max.d <- 100

true.mean.N <- matrix(data = 0, nrow = max.d, 
                           ncol = length(P))

for (c in 1:length(P)){
  for (d in 1:max.d){
    true.mean.N[d, c] <- floor(Richards_fcn(d = d, 
                                            S1 = -S1, 
                                            S2 = S2,
                                            K = K, 
                                            P = P[c], 
                                            min = 0, max = max.N)  )
    
  }
  
}

data.df <- data.frame(Day = rep(1:max.d, times = length(P)),
                      mean.N = as.vector(true.mean.N),
                      P = rep(P, each = max.d))

p.P <- ggplot(data = data.df) +
  geom_path(aes(x = Day, y = mean.N), color = "red") +
  facet_wrap(~ P) +
  xlab("Ordinal Day of Season") +
  ylab("") +
  theme_bw()

save.fig.fcn(p.P, 
             file.name = paste0("figures/P_", 
                                dpi.set, "dpi.png"), 
             replace = T, 
             height = 5, width = 7,dpi = dpi.set)


```

```{r plot-P-effects, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE}

knitr::include_graphics(paste0("figures/P_", dpi.set, "dpi.png"))
fig.P.num <- run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", 
                          pre_label = "Figure A", 
                          bkm = "Figure-P",
                          start_at = 1)

```

::: {custom-style="Image Caption"}
`r run_autonum(seq_id = "figA", pre_label = "Figure A", bkm = "Figure-P")` Effects of $P$ on the shape of the Richards equation. In this example, $S_1$ = `r S1`, $S_2$ = `r S2`, $\gamma$ = `r K`, $N_{max}$ = `r max.N`.
:::
